content.text: Function as a Service (FaaS) is a cloud execution model in which you deploy individual functions rather than long-running servers. You write a handler function, configure a trigger (an HTTP request, an S3 upload, a queue message), and the cloud provider executes your function on demand — provisioning compute, routing the event, and billing you only for the milliseconds your code actually runs.

. Lambda is the foundation of serverless on AWS and integrates with over 200 AWS services as event sources. The broader "serverless" term also includes managed services that require no server provisioning: API Gateway (HTTP routing), DynamoDB (database), S3 (object storage), SQS/SNS (messaging), and Step Functions (orchestration).

FaaS sits beyond PaaS on the abstraction spectrum. PaaS gives you a platform to deploy an always-running application; FaaS gives you compute that exists only for the duration of a single invocation. There are no idle servers, no autoscaling policies to write, and no capacity to reserve — the platform scales from zero to thousands of concurrent executions automatically.

The diagram below shows the event-driven architecture around Lambda. Events arrive from over 200 trigger types on the left; Lambda executes your function in an isolated environment and calls downstream services on the right.

attrs.alt: AWS FaaS serverless event-driven architecture showing event sources, Lambda, and downstream services
attrs.title: AWS Lambda Event-Driven Architecture

content.text: Each Lambda invocation runs in a sandboxed

— a lightweight MicroVM powered by Firecracker (AWS's open-source VMM). The environment is initialized once (cold start), then reused for subsequent invocations from the same function (warm invocations). When concurrent demand exceeds available warm environments, Lambda transparently initializes new ones.

Lambda bills per invocation and per GB-second of execution. A function that runs 1 million times per month at 200ms and 128 MB costs approximately $0.40 — and $0.00 when unused. There are no instance-hours to pay when traffic is zero.
Lambda scales concurrency automatically. A sudden burst from 1 to 3,000 concurrent requests is handled without any scaling policy, capacity reservation, or health check warmup.
AWS provisions, patches, and retires the underlying EC2/Firecracker fleet. You never SSH into a Lambda host.

Lambda integrates natively with over 200 AWS services as triggers. Adding a processing step to any event stream (S3 uploads, DynamoDB changes, SQS queues) requires adding a single Lambda trigger — no polling infrastructure.
Managed runtimes for Node.js 20.x, Python 3.12, Java 21, .NET 8, Go 1.x, and Ruby 3.3. Container image deployment (up to 10 GB) supports any language or binary through a custom OS-only base image.
Lambda runs across multiple Availability Zones within a region automatically. You do not configure multi-AZ; it is the default.

Initializing a new execution environment takes 100ms–2s depending on runtime and package size. During a cold start, the handler init code runs before the first invocation is processed. This adds latency that is invisible on warm invocations but affects tail latency on bursty workloads.

Lambda cannot run longer than 15 minutes per invocation. Long-running jobs (ETL, video transcoding, model training) must be split into chains of shorter functions using Step Functions or moved to batch compute.
is writable (512 MB default, up to 10 GB), and it is not shared between execution environments. Shared state must go to S3, DynamoDB, or ElastiCache.

The default regional concurrency limit is 1,000. A traffic spike that exceeds 1,000 concurrent invocations triggers throttling (429 errors). Increase the limit via a service quota request and set reserved concurrency per function to prevent one function from consuming the entire regional pool.

Functions attached to a VPC need an ENI (Elastic Network Interface) for VPC access. ENI creation previously caused significant cold starts; this improved with Hyperplane ENIs in 2019, but VPC functions still initialize marginally slower than non-VPC functions.
Cost inversion at high, steady throughput.
At very high constant request rates, Lambda's per-invocation pricing can exceed the cost of a dedicated EC2 or ECS fleet. The serverless price advantage is greatest for variable or bursty workloads.
Lambda Execution Model — Cold vs Warm
Understanding the Lambda execution environment lifecycle is essential for writing performant serverless code.

Lambda starts a new Firecracker MicroVM, downloads your deployment package, starts the language runtime, and runs your initialization code (everything outside the handler function). SDK clients, database connections, and loaded config files initialized here are reused across warm invocations.

Lambda passes the event to your handler function and waits for it to return or throw. The environment persists for subsequent invocations to the same instance for minutes to hours depending on traffic. Variables initialized in module scope survive across invocations in the same environment.
If no invocations arrive for a period, Lambda freezes and eventually terminates the environment. Extension-registered shutdown hooks receive a SIGTERM with a brief window to flush telemetry.

The practical implication: place expensive setup code (SDK client construction, config loading, database connection pool initialization) at module scope so it runs once per cold start, not once per invocation.
How to Implement — Your First Lambda Function

import json
import boto3
from datetime import datetime, timezone

# Module-scope initialization — runs ONCE per cold start, reused on warm invocations
dynamodb = boto3.resource('dynamodb')
table = dynamodb.Table('Orders')

def handler(event, context):
    """
    event: the trigger payload (shape depends on source — API Gateway,
           S3, SQS, etc.)
    context: Lambda runtime metadata (function name, remaining time, etc.)
    """
    # For an API Gateway proxy integration, the body is a JSON string
    body = json.loads(event.get('body', '{}'))
    order_id = body.get('orderId')

    if not order_id:
        return {
            'statusCode': 400,
            'body': json.dumps({'error': 'orderId is required'})
        }

    # Write to DynamoDB — uses the reused client from module scope
    table.put_item(Item={
        'orderId': order_id,
        'status': 'received',
        'receivedAt': datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
    })

    return {
        'statusCode': 200,
        'headers': {'Content-Type': 'application/json'},
        'body': json.dumps({'orderId': order_id, 'status': 'received'})
    }
Step 2 — Deploy with the AWS CLI

# Package the function
zip function.zip handler.py

# Create an IAM execution role (one-time setup)
TRUST='{"Version":"2012-10-17","Statement":[{"Effect":"Allow","Principal":{"Service":"lambda.amazonaws.com"},"Action":"sts:AssumeRole"}]}'
ROLE_ARN=$(aws iam create-role \
  --role-name order-receiver-role \
  --assume-role-policy-document "$TRUST" \
  --query Role.Arn --output text)

# Attach managed policies
aws iam attach-role-policy \
  --role-name order-receiver-role \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole

# Give Lambda access to the Orders DynamoDB table
aws iam put-role-policy \
  --role-name order-receiver-role \
  --policy-name dynamodb-orders-write \
  --policy-document '{
    "Version":"2012-10-17",
    "Statement":[{
      "Effect":"Allow",
      "Action":["dynamodb:PutItem"],
      "Resource":"arn:aws:dynamodb:us-east-1:123456789012:table/Orders"
    }]
  }'

sleep 10  # Wait for role propagation

# Create the function
aws lambda create-function \
  --function-name order-receiver \
  --runtime python3.12 \
  --handler handler.handler \
  --zip-file fileb://function.zip \
  --role $ROLE_ARN \
  --memory-size 256 \
  --timeout 30 \
  --region us-east-1
Step 3 — Connect an API Gateway Trigger

# Create an HTTP API (API Gateway v2 — lower latency, lower cost than REST API)
API_ID=$(aws apigatewayv2 create-api \
  --name orders-api \
  --protocol-type HTTP \
  --query ApiId --output text)

# Create a Lambda integration
INTEGRATION_ID=$(aws apigatewayv2 create-integration \
  --api-id $API_ID \
  --integration-type AWS_PROXY \
  --integration-uri arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789012:function:order-receiver \
  --payload-format-version 2.0 \
  --query IntegrationId --output text)

# Create a route
aws apigatewayv2 create-route \
  --api-id $API_ID \
  --route-key 'POST /orders' \
  --target "integrations/$INTEGRATION_ID"

# Deploy to a $default stage (auto-deploy enabled)
aws apigatewayv2 create-stage \
  --api-id $API_ID \
  --stage-name '$default' \
  --auto-deploy

# Grant API Gateway permission to invoke the Lambda
aws lambda add-permission \
  --function-name order-receiver \
  --statement-id apigw-invoke \
  --action lambda:InvokeFunction \
  --principal apigateway.amazonaws.com

echo "API endpoint: https://$API_ID.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/orders"

# Edit handler.py, then update and publish a new version
zip function.zip handler.py

aws lambda update-function-code \
  --function-name order-receiver \
  --zip-file fileb://function.zip

# Publish an immutable version (used for blue/green via aliases)
VERSION=$(aws lambda publish-version \
  --function-name order-receiver \
  --query Version --output text)

echo "Published version $VERSION"

Provisioned Concurrency pre-initializes execution environments so they are warm and ready before any invocation arrives. This eliminates cold start latency for latency-sensitive workloads at the cost of an hourly charge for the provisioned environments.

# Pre-initialize 5 execution environments for the production alias
aws lambda put-provisioned-concurrency-config \
  --function-name order-receiver \
  --qualifier production \
  --provisioned-concurrent-executions 5

# Provisioned concurrency pricing: ~$0.0000646 per GB-second provisioned
# For 5 environments at 256 MB = 1.25 GB provisioned:
# 1.25 * $0.0000646 * 3600 seconds * 24 hours = ~$6.94/day
# Compare this to p99 latency improvements before deciding
A photo-sharing application needs to generate thumbnails for every image upload. A Lambda-based pipeline handles this without any servers:
User uploads an image to an S3 bucket (
Lambda reads the image from S3, uses Pillow (bundled in a Lambda Layer) to generate 3 thumbnail sizes, and writes them to
Lambda writes thumbnail metadata to DynamoDB and publishes a completion message to SNS, which fans out to SQS queues consumed by downstream services (CDN invalidation, user notification).

Throughput scales automatically: if 10,000 photos are uploaded simultaneously, Lambda spins up 10,000 concurrent execution environments (subject to account concurrency limits). Cost is proportional to actual processing: 100 ms per thumbnail × 10,000 images = 1,000 seconds of compute, billed at roughly $0.02 at 512 MB memory.
Opening database connections inside the handler.

Each invocation that opens a new PostgreSQL connection creates a connection to the database. At 1,000 concurrent Lambda invocations this exhausts typical RDS max_connections. Initialize the connection pool at module scope and use RDS Proxy to multiplex connections from thousands of Lambda environments into a small connection pool.
Storing state between invocations in memory.
Global variables do persist between warm invocations of the

execution environment, but a different concurrent invocation runs in a different environment with its own memory. Never rely on in-process state for coordination between requests — use DynamoDB, ElastiCache, or SQS.

The default Lambda timeout is 3 seconds. If your function makes an external API call that occasionally takes 2.8 seconds, you are already near the limit. Set the timeout to 2–3× your expected p99 execution time, accounting for cold starts on the first invocation.

Lambda deployment packages over 50 MB (zipped) or 250 MB (unzipped) cannot be deployed directly — they must use S3 or a container image. Large packages also increase cold start time. Exclude dev dependencies from production packages and use Lambda Layers for shared dependencies.
Ignoring SQS visibility timeout vs Lambda timeout.

When Lambda processes SQS messages, the SQS visibility timeout must be at least 6× the Lambda function timeout. If Lambda times out, SQS makes the message visible again after the visibility timeout expires. If the visibility timeout is shorter than the Lambda timeout, the same message is processed multiple times concurrently.
Lambda with SQS, SNS, or Kinesis provides at-least-once delivery. Your handler may be invoked more than once with the same event. Use conditional DynamoDB writes (
) or check a processed-events table to handle duplicates safely.
Initialize expensive clients at module scope.
boto3 clients, DB connections, loaded config, and parsed secrets should be initialized once outside the handler. Each reuse of a warm environment gets these for free.
Tune memory to the right size.

Lambda's vCPU allocation scales proportionally with memory. Doubling memory from 512 MB to 1024 MB often halves execution time for CPU-bound functions, resulting in the same GB-second cost but faster responses. Use AWS Lambda Power Tuning to find the optimal memory setting.
Use environment variables for configuration; Secrets Manager for secrets.
Never hardcode API keys or database passwords. Retrieve secrets at cold start using
and cache the result in a module-scope variable.
Configure a DLQ (SQS queue) for async Lambda invocations. If your function fails all retry attempts, the event is sent to the DLQ for later inspection and replay rather than silently dropped.
Deploy with SAM or CDK, not direct CLI.

The AWS SAM CLI (Serverless Application Model) provides local Lambda emulation, packaging, and deployment in a repeatable template. CDK constructs for Lambda add sane defaults for environment variables, IAM roles, and log retention.
), and deploy Lambda applications with a single command (
— define Lambda functions, layers, event sources, and IAM policies as TypeScript or Python code with full IDE completion and type safety.
— an open-source Step Functions state machine that benchmarks your function at every memory tier and plots cost vs. performance to find the optimal configuration.
— distributed tracing for Lambda. Annotate your function with the X-Ray SDK and see end-to-end traces from API Gateway through Lambda to DynamoDB, including cold start segments.

— orchestrate multi-step serverless workflows. When a task exceeds Lambda's 15-minute limit, Step Functions can model it as a sequence of shorter Lambda invocations with built-in retry logic, branching, and error handling.

Think of Lambda as a vending machine for compute. You put in a coin (the event), the machine dispenses exactly what you ordered (your function runs), and then the machine goes idle. You are not paying for the vending machine to sit there between purchases — only for each item dispensed.

The constraint: you cannot store anything in the vending machine between customers (ephemeral runtime), and orders must complete in under 15 minutes (timeout). For longer work or stateful workflows, combine multiple vending machines in a sequence — that is Step Functions.
Lambda security has two axes: controlling what your function
Every Lambda function assumes an IAM role at invocation time. That role determines which AWS APIs the function can call. The most common mistake is attaching
on a single table. Scope every policy to the minimum action and the specific resource ARN.

Lambda encrypts environment variables at rest using a KMS key (AWS-managed by default; BYOK for stricter compliance). For sensitive values — API keys, database passwords, OAuth tokens — use Secrets Manager rather than environment variables. Retrieve the secret at module scope (cold start only) and cache it:

import boto3, json, os

_sm = boto3.client('secretsmanager')

# Cached at module scope — one Secrets Manager call per cold start
_secret_cache = None

def get_secret():
    global _secret_cache
    if _secret_cache is None:
        response = _sm.get_secret_value(SecretId=os.environ['DB_SECRET_ARN'])
        _secret_cache = json.loads(response['SecretString'])
    return _secret_cache

def handler(event, context):
    creds = get_secret()  # Fast on warm invocations
    # ... use creds['username'] and creds['password']
A Lambda resource policy controls who can invoke the function. API Gateway, S3, and EventBridge add a
principal statement automatically when you create a trigger. Lambda Function URLs (a direct HTTPS endpoint on the function, without API Gateway) support two auth modes:
(public, use only when you implement your own auth inside the handler, e.g., HMAC webhook validation).

Lambda automatically publishes logs to CloudWatch Logs and emits built-in metrics (invocations, errors, duration, throttles, concurrent executions) to CloudWatch Metrics. Adding structured logging and X-Ray tracing significantly improves debuggability.
Lambda captures everything written to stdout/stderr and sends it to CloudWatch Logs under
. Use JSON-structured logs so CloudWatch Logs Insights can filter by field, and include the
from the context object in every log entry for correlation:

import json, logging, time

logger = logging.getLogger()
logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)

def log(level, msg, request_id, **kwargs):
    record = {"level": level, "message": msg, "requestId": request_id, **kwargs}
    print(json.dumps(record))  # Lambda captures stdout to CloudWatch

def handler(event, context):
    start = time.perf_counter()
    log("INFO", "Invocation started", context.aws_request_id,
        source=event.get("source"), remainingMs=context.get_remaining_time_in_millis())

    try:
        result = process(event)
        duration_ms = (time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000
        log("INFO", "Invocation completed", context.aws_request_id,
            durationMs=round(duration_ms, 2))
        return result
    except Exception as exc:
        log("ERROR", str(exc), context.aws_request_id, exc_type=type(exc).__name__)
        raise
— alarm on any non-zero error count for critical functions (SUM of
— throttling means the 1,000 concurrency limit (or your reserved limit) was hit. Requests were rejected.
— when p99 execution time approaches the timeout, occasional invocations will hit the limit and be force-killed.
— measures how far behind the stream processor is. Rising iterator age indicates Lambda cannot keep up with the stream shard throughput.
Enable active tracing in the Lambda configuration to record end-to-end traces including cold start segments and downstream calls:

aws lambda update-function-configuration \
  --function-name order-receiver \
  --tracing-config Mode=Active

# In Python, instrument boto3 automatically:
pip install aws-xray-sdk

# handler.py
from aws_xray_sdk.core import xray_recorder, patch_all
patch_all()  # Instruments boto3, requests, sqlalchemy, etc.

@xray_recorder.capture('process_order')
def process(event):
    # Subsegments appear in the X-Ray trace as named spans
    with xray_recorder.in_subsegment('validate') as sub:
        sub.put_annotation('orderId', event['orderId'])
        validate(event)
    # ...

X-Ray Service Maps show a visual graph of all services your function called (DynamoDB, S3, external HTTP) and the latency distribution at each hop. Cold start segments are labeled "Initialization" in the trace and are typically the longest segment on the first invocation of each execution environment.
Lambda Layers — Sharing Code and Dependencies
A Lambda Layer is a ZIP archive containing libraries, a custom runtime, or binary dependencies that multiple functions can reference. Layers are extracted to
at cold start. Your deployment package does not need to bundle the same dependencies repeatedly.

# Build a Pillow layer for Python 3.12 (x86_64)
mkdir -p python/lib/python3.12/site-packages
pip install Pillow -t python/lib/python3.12/site-packages --platform manylinux2014_x86_64 \
  --only-binary=:all: --python-version 3.12
zip -r pillow-layer.zip python/

# Publish the layer
LAYER_ARN=$(aws lambda publish-layer-version \
  --layer-name pillow-layer \
  --zip-file fileb://pillow-layer.zip \
  --compatible-runtimes python3.12 \
  --query LayerVersionArn --output text)

# Attach to a function
aws lambda update-function-configuration \
  --function-name thumbnail-generator \
  --layers $LAYER_ARN

# In handler.py — Pillow is now available at /opt/python/...
from PIL import Image

Each function can attach up to 5 layers. The total unzipped size of the function code plus all layers cannot exceed 250 MB. AWS and community-maintained layers are available in the Serverless Application Repository for common packages (pandas, Pillow, PyArrow, Chromium headless, AWS Lambda Powertools).
Comparing Serverless to PaaS and IaaS
FaaS/serverless is event-driven compute billed per invocation with no idle cost and automatic scaling from zero.
AWS Lambda is the FaaS primitive. It integrates with 200+ event sources (API Gateway, S3, SQS, DynamoDB Streams, EventBridge, Kinesis) out of the box.
Lambda execution environments are reused (warm) or initialized fresh (cold start). Place expensive init code at module scope to amortize cold start cost.
Key constraints: 15-minute max timeout, 1,000 default concurrent executions, ephemeral /tmp only, no persistent state in memory across environments.
Write idempotent handlers — Lambda delivers events at-least-once from SQS, SNS, and Kinesis.

Use Provisioned Concurrency to eliminate cold starts for latency-sensitive functions, RDS Proxy to manage database connections at scale, and Step Functions to orchestrate workflows beyond the 15-minute limit.
